title: K. Koteshwar Reddy vs Madhya Pradesh Power Generating Comany ... on 17 February, 2026
body: K. Koteshwar Reddy vs Madhya Pradesh Power Generating Comany ... on 17 February, 2026 1 MP-971-2026 IN THE HIGH COURT OF MADHYA PRADESH AT JABALPUR BEFORE HON'BLE SHRI JUSTICE VIVEK JAIN ON THE 17th OF FEBRUARY, 2026 MISC. PETITION No. 971 of 2026 K. KOTESHWAR REDDY Versus MADHYA PRADESH POWER GENERATING COMANY LIMITED AND OTHERS Appearance: Shri Ravi Shankar Saini - Advocate for the petitioner. ORDER By way of this petition, the petitioner/defendant No. 1 has challenged the impugned order passed by the trial court dated 03.02.2026 whereby the trial court has rejected the application of the petitioner under Section 151 C.P.C. to record his deposition in Telugu language by appointing one Shri P. Mahadeva Reddy, Resident of Hyderabad, (Telangana) as interpreter/translator. The aforesaid request has been turned down by the trial court. 2. The petitioner is defendant No. 1 in a suit filed by the plaintiff, which is Power Generating Company of State of Madhya Pradesh, on the ground that a contract for removal of fly ash was given to the present petitioner. On account of certain lapses alleged to be made on part of the present petitioner, the plaintiff has filed a suit for recovery of money to the tune of Rs. 96.16 lakhs, along with interest. 3. When the case came up for defendant evidence, then an Signature Not Verified Signed by: ARVIND KUMAR MISHRA Signing time: 23-02-2026 18:55:59 2 MP-971-2026 application was filed under Section 151 C.P.C. by the petitioner/ defendant No. 1 that he has studied in Telugu medium and he can only understand Telugu language. He does not understand or speak Hindi or English. Since the court is working in Hindi language, therefore, it is not possible for him to give his deposition unless one Shri P. Mahadeva Reddy is appointed as interpreter/translator to translate his deposition given in Telugu to Hindi or English in the record of the court. 4. The trial court has rejected the said request on the ground that the official language of the court is Hindi as per notification issued by State of Madhya Pradesh and as per Rule 8(a) of MP Civil Court Rules 1961, therefore, the deposition of the petitioner cannot be recorded in Telugu and even the court does not understand Telugu language. 5. Upon considering the aforesaid dispute, it is seen that the petitioner had entered into an agreement with Power Generating Utility of State of Madhya Pradesh. He was performing the contract in Madhya Pradesh and Madhya Pradesh being a Hindi speaking State, it cannot be inferred that a person executing a contract in the State of Madhya Pradesh is not convergent with Hindi language and is not convergent even with English language. In Madhya Pradesh, Telugu language is not spoken and a person who is not conversant with Hindi or even English would find it difficult at least to do business by taking a contract in Madhya Pradesh which involves employing labourers and dealing with common people which cannot be done without having a working knowledge of Hindi, at least in Madhya Pradesh. A Contractor taking up inter-State contract in Hindi speaking State like Signature Not Verified Signed by: ARVIND KUMAR MISHRA Signing time: 23-02-2026 18:55:59 3 MP-971-2026 Madhya Pradesh, cannot bonafidely state that he knows only Telugu and Court should proceed in Telugu, or he should get interpreter of his choice. 6. As per section 137 of Code of Procedure procedure, the language of subordinate court shall be the language of the High Court unless the State Government otherwise directs. For some limited purposes, English can be utilized by the Trial Court as per Section 137(3) and in case of inability of the party or its pleader to understand English, then the translated version of English pleadings have to be supplied as per Section 137(3) , which would be in "language of Court", which in Madhya Pradesh, is Hindi. 7. As per Order 18 Rule 6 CPC , when evidence is taken down in a language different from that in which it is given and the witness does not understand the language in which it is taken down, the evidence as taken down in writing shall be interpreted to him in the language in which it is given. The petitioner therefore seeks in essence, seeks benefit of Order 18 Rule 6 CPC , though the application is captioned as one under Section 151 CPC. 8. The relevant Sections 137 , 138 and Order 18 Rule 6 CPC are as under:- 137. Language of subordinate Courts.-(1) The language which, on the commencement of this Code, is the language of any Court subordinate to a High Court shall continue to be the language of such subordinate Court until the State Government otherwise directs. (2) The State Government may declare what shall be the language of any such Court and in what character applications to and proceedings in such Courts shall be written. (3) Where this Court requires or allows anything other that the recording of evidence to be done in writing in any such Court, such writing may be in Signature Not Verified Signed by: ARVIND KUMAR MISHRA Signing time: 23-02-2026 18:55:59 4 MP-971-2026 English; but if any party or his pleader is unacquainted with English a translation into the language of the Court shall, at his request, be supplied to him; and the Court shall make such order as it thinks fit in respect of the payment of the costs of such translation. [138. Power of High Court to require evidence to be recorded in English.-(1) The [High Court] may, by notification in the Official Gazette, direct with respect to any Judge specified in the notification, or falling under a description set forth therein, that evidence in cases in which an appeal is allowed shall be taken down by him in the English language and in manner prescribed. (2) Where a Judge is prevented by any sufficient reason from complying with a direction under sub-section (1), he shall record the reason and cause the evidence to be taken down in writing from his dictation in open Court. 18(6). When deposition to be interpreted. Where the evidence is taken down in a language different from that in which it is given, and the witness does not understand the language in which it is taken down, the evidence as taken down in writing shall be interpreted to him in the language in which it is given. 9. In the present case the petitioner is not entitled to benefit of Order 18 Rule 6 CPC also and he is in fact seeking the benefit of this provision because he seeks that he should depose in Telugu and the interpreter will interpret the deposition in English or Hindi so that it can be recorded in the court in Hindi or English. 10. For that purpose it is to be seen whether the request of the petitioner is bona fide and whether it can be made in terms of Section 137 CPC. 11. Though Order 18 Rule 6 CPC does not prohibit use of any other language than the language of the Court, but it is to be seen whether the request of the petitioner is bona fide. In the present case as already noted above the petitioner had entered into a contract to remove fly ash cenosphere from a thermal power plant in Madhya Pradesh. He being a businessman Signature Not Verified Signed by: ARVIND KUMAR MISHRA Signing time: 23-02-2026 18:55:59 5 MP-971-2026 operating across the States, even in Hindi speaking State, cannot state that he is not conversant with English and Hindi and he is conversant only in Telugu language. The request is nothing but an attempt to permit one Shri P. Mahadev Reddy to in fact depose on behalf of the petitioner, that cannot be permitted. The Trial court is not conversant with Telugu language and allowing deposition to be recorded in such cases in Telugu language would be subversive to this case. Therefore the impugned order of the trial court is upheld. The petitioner shall be granted option to get his deposition recorded either in Hindi or in English. 12. With the aforesaid observation, the petition stands dismissed. (VIVEK JAIN) JUDGE MISHRA Signature Not Verified Signed by: ARVIND KUMAR MISHRA Signing time: 23-02-2026 18:55:59
